paper_title: Proliferator-Activated Receptor Alpha Inhibits Abnormal Extracellular Matrix Accumulation and Maintains Energy Metabolism in Late-Onset Fuchs Endothelial Corneal Dystrophy.
raw_limitation_text:
[DISCUSSION]
The corneal endothelium is the key tissue responsible for maintaining corneal transparency, with its primary function being the transportation of nutrients from the aqueous humor to the cornea and the regulation of fluid from the stroma to the anterior chamber. CECs have a high demand for ATP; thus, the density of mitochondria in the corneal endothelium ranks second only to that of photoreceptor cells. This study for the first time proposes that the mitochondrial FAO–TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation are significantly impaired in FECD through GSEA analysis of GSE171830. Furthermore, the key downregulated gene of energy metabolism in the FECD dataset, PPARα, primarily regulates the FAO process. Through in vivo experiments involving PPARα−/− mice, PPARα overexpression, and activation of PPARα by fenofibrate, it was demonstrated that PPARα exerts a protective effect on the corneal endothelium and can inhibit abnormal thickening of the Descemet membrane.

A meta-analysis of RNA sequencing datasets of FECD corneal endothelium demonstrated that both mitochondrial functions and energy-related processes were decreased in FECD cases with or without the CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion. Except the OXPHOS decreasing in FECD cases, our study also marks the first observation in FECD cases of decreased mitochondrial FAO process, which conducted the acetyl-coenzyme A carboxylase for TCA cycle and OXPHOS. Previous studies related to mitochondrial function and energy production in corneal endothelium mostly focus on the function of SLC4A11. Hamuro et al. reported that MiR-34a induced the polarization of mitochondrial respiration to OXPHOS, after cell-state transition. In our study, an analysis of all genes involved in energy metabolism revealed that PPARα is the most significantly downregulated gene in FECD. We generated PPARα−/− mice and found that there were mild corneal opacity and endothelial damage in aged PPARα−/− mice. Under the same intensity of UV-A irradiation, the corneal opacity in aged PPARα−/− mice was heavier and corneas were thicker than that in wild-type C57BL/6 mice. For ZO-1 staining and in vivo confocal microscopy, the number of regular hexagonal CECs in PPARα−/− mice was significantly lower than that in wild-type mice. In addition, the corneal metabolites changes of PPARα−/− mice collaborating with enzymes alteration in FECD cases of GSE171830. Overexpression by intracameral injection AAV-shPPARα or activation of PPARα by feeding 0.2% fenofibrate feed alleviated corneal endothelial damage after irradiation, shown as maintaining cell density and cell morphology, reducing corneal edema and opacity. However, PPARα overexpression or activation had a limited effect on the loss of microvillus and the enlargement of intercellular spaces. Thus, we identified that PPARα was a protective factor for corneal endothelium in FECD.

The fibrotic process associated with guttae formation is the key factor leading to the decrease in CEC density in FECD. Currently, there is no method to suppress guttae generation. In our study, we observed that PPARα can inhibit the thickening of Descemet membrane in a UV-A–irradiated mice late-onset FECD model. Using GSVA, we found a negative correlation between corneal endothelial fibrosis and the FAO process. Furthermore, we observed that fenofibrate or overexpression of PPARα significantly inhibited the thickening of the Descemet membrane and the accumulation of abnormal fibers in Descemet membrane induced by UV-A irradiation. After irradiation, the expression of expression of α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA) and Col1a1 in 0.2% fenofibrate fed mice was decreased, thereby inhibiting the process of corneal fibrosis. In addition, PPARα can protect the mitochondria from injury, increase mitochondria production and active the FAO process. Fenofibrate treatment in B4G12 reduced the destruction of the mitochondrial membrane potential induced by TGF-β. Mitochondrial biosynthesis related gene, Tfam, was decreased after irradiation; compared with the regular feed group, Tfam was increased in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group before or after irradiation. After irradiation, the FAO-related enzymes, Cpt1a and ACADM, were decreased in the regular feed group, whereas they were increased in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group compared with control group. Fenofibrate can also increase the maximal oxygen consumption induced by FCCP with or without TGF-β treatment. Previous studies have reported that regulating key enzymes in the FAO process can inhibit TGF-β-induced fibrosis. Chung et al. reported that the exaggerated lipid accumulated in kidneys of aged PPARα−/− mice with decreased activity of the FAO pathway and a severe fibrosis. Gallage et al. reported that the combined knockdown of PPARα and Pck1 in vivo abolished the beneficial outcomes of fasting against inflammation and fibrosis via activation of a PI3K/AKT/PDGF axis. Moreover, overexpression of Pck1 alone or together with PPARα in vivo lowered hepatic triglycerides and steatosis. Both of these experiments were consistent with the phenomenon we have observed in corneal endothelium. In our study, we sought to explain how PPARα inhibits the fibrosis process. We tested the expression of S100A4 in our study, a damage-associated molecular pattern protein involved in fibrotic responses. After irradiation, the expression of S100A4 was increased with or without fenofibrate treatment. This finding indicates that S100A4 is not the target of PPARα in inhibiting the fibrosis of endothelium.

The cellular cytoskeleton is a crucial structure responsible for sensing and regulating the ECM. It serves as a mediator for the interaction between cells and the ECM, facilitating the deposition, rearrangement, and degradation of the ECM by transmitting signals into the cell. The dynamic structure of the cytoskeleton is affected by changes in energy supply, and the physiological function of cells depends on the balance between the assembly and disassembly of the cytoskeleton with other types of proteins. An insufficient energy supply disrupts the balance of cytoskeletal signaling, leading to the deposition and structural disorganization of the ECM. In our study, after irradiation, compared with the AAV-shNC group, overexpression of corneal endothelial PPARα resulted in a more regular arrangement of cell–cell connections as observed by scanning electron microscopy, with no apparent disruption of cell connections. Additionally, in vitro experiments demonstrated that administration of fenofibrate significantly inhibited the occurrence of fibrosis induced by TGF-β. E-cad and N-cad are important factors in maintaining the pentagonal-hexagonal morphology of CECs. Previous studies have reported that, during CEC fibrosis, E-cad and N-cad redistribute from the cell membrane to the cytoplasm. In vitro experiments showed that the administration of fenofibrate inhibited the redistribution of E-cad and N-cad to the cytoplasm, which was induced by TGF-β.

Although PPARα activation partially mitigates mitochondrial dysfunction and fibrosis, there still were some limitations of PPARα activation as a therapeutic strategy. First, PPARα activation did not preserve the microvilli structure and cell–cell junctions fully. In future treatments, it may be possible to combine this agent with the other drugs, which could save microvilli loss and increase intercellular space. In addition, the development of topical ocular administration of fenofibrate will avoid the liver and kidney toxicity of oral administration.

In conclusion, our study demonstrates that the decline of PPARα is an important reason for the occurrence of FECD. Perturbation of PPARα signaling may disturb mitochondria fatty acid oxidation (FAO) process, then cause corneal endothelium fibrosis and producing abnormal ECM in Descemet membrane (Fig. 9D). Overexpression or activation of PPARα can inhibit the thickening of Descemet membrane, which is the primary pathology in FECD.

Fenofibrate protected mitochondrial integrity and mitochondrial FAO in B4G12 with TGF-β treatment. We treated B4G12 cells with 10 ng/mL of TGF-β for 24 h to induce fibrosis, and the TGF-β + Feno group received 30 µM fenofibrate simultaneous. The groups were divided into control, Feno, TGF-β, and TGF-β + Feno groups. (A) JC1 staining of B4G12 in different groups. JC-1 monomers, escaping to cytobolism from mitochondria, was shown as green, JC-1 aggregates was shown as red. (B) Histogram of JC-1 monomer positive cell calculation (per view). (C) Line graph of maximum oxygen consumption induced by FCCP. n = 3, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

Supplementary Material

[CONCLUSION]
Overall, we suggested that the overexpression or activation of PPARα can inhibit FAO energy dysfunction of corneal endothelium and the abnormal extracellular matrix formation in Descemet's membrane, which is the primary pathology of FECD. Thus, PPARα may be a potential target for attenuating the progression of FECD.

Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy (FECD) is the most common cause of corneal endothelial decompensation. It is a degenerative disorder resulting from a combination of genetic mutations and environmental factors. Late-onset FECD, typically affecting individuals aged 50 to 60 years, is an age-related disorder that manifests in the formation of abnormal extracellular matrix (ECM) deposits, known as guttae, and consequent the apoptosis of corneal endothelial cells (CECs). In patients with FECD, the Descemet membrane thickness is four times that of normal individuals, with the posterior nonbanded layer thinning or disappearing and being replaced by the abnormal collagenous banded layer. Guttae are crucial in causing progressive loss of corneal endothelium, with endothelial cells near guttae exhibiting varying degrees of degeneration under electron microscopy. Therefore, finding a way to inhibit the formation of guttae is the key to preventing CEC apoptosis and subsequent endothelial decompensation.

Insufficient energy supply can disrupt the balance of cellular cytoskeleton, leading to the deposition and structural disruption of the ECM. CECs are among highly energy-demanding cells, with mitochondrial density ranking second only to photoreceptor cells among various cell types in the body. Mitochondria fatty acid β-oxidation (FAO), generating large amounts of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), serves as the primary energy source for highly energy-demanding cells. In patients with FECD, there exists an abnormal lipid metabolism within the corneal endothelium and its surrounding environment. A study revealed an increase in cholesterols and phospholipids in the aqueous humor of patients with FECD compared with age-matched controls. A literature search found that lipid deposited in the cornea stroma of the FECD group. In patients with FECD, there is a decrease in apolipoprotein E in the corneal endothelium and Descemet membrane. Hence, there may be abnormalities in fatty acid uptake and metabolism in FECD.

In this study, gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) analysis of lipid metabolism processes in the GSE171830 dataset revealed a significant decrease in mitochondria FAO in patients with FECD. Decreases in the FAO–tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle and oxidative phosphorylation processes were also found in patients of GSE171830with FECD. We further identified proliferator–activated receptor alpha (PPARα) as a core regulatory factor among these energy production processes. The regulation of PPARα expression and lipid metabolism can lead to severe fibrosis. Chung et al. reported that the exaggerated lipid accumulated in kidneys of aged PPARα−/− mice with decreased activity of the FAO pathway and a severe fibrosis. Gallage et al. reported deletion of Pck1 and PPARα exacerbates nonalcoholic steatohepatitis. We hypothesize that the process of FAO metabolism during FECD may be a key factor leading to the deposition of ECM and guttae formation. In this study, we revealed that aged PPARα−/− mice presented CECs enlargement and diversity as well as cornea opacity and thickening, mimicking features of late-onset FECD. Metabolomics analysis of corneal tissue from aged PPARα−/− mice showed a decrease in FAO-related metabolites, consistent with the downregulation of FAO enzymes observed in the GSE171830 dataset. Then, feeding fenofibrate, the PPARα agonist, and intracameral injection of adeno-associated virus (AAV)-shPPARα were used to treat a late-onset FECD mice model induced by UV-A irradiation. We found that the overexpression or activation of PPARα demonstrated reduced corneal endothelial damage and reversal of Descemet's membrane thickening after irradiation, along with downregulation of fibrosis-related genes such as α-SMA and collagen type I alpha 1 (Col1a1). In vitro experiments revealed that fenofibrate could reverse fibrosis induced by TGF-β and mitigate damage to cell morphology and cell-to-cell connections. Additionally, fenofibrate was found to alleviate mitochondrial damage in CECs and increase oxygen consumption rates after TGF-β treatment.

Methods

Data Acquisition and Preprocessing

The microarray dataset GSE171830 was obtained from the GEO database. GSE171830 consists of corneal endothelium cRNA samples from three control and nine FECD specimens, deposited in GPL10558 (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA; HumanHT-12 V4.0 expression bead chip). Preprocessing of raw data was run by R (4.1.3) and RStudio, including TPM transformation, quantile normalization, and log2 transformation based on the Biobase, limma, and sva packages in particular.

GSEA

GSEA is a computational method that determines whether a gene set has statistically significant and concordant differences between two biological states. GSEA was performed on the gene expression matrix of GSE171830 using the clusterProfiler package (4.0). The subsets of lipid metabolism (R-HSA-556833), TCA cycle, and respiratory electron transport chain (R-HSA-1428517) in the Reactome database were analyzed by GSEA. The subsets of lipid metabolism consist of fatty acid metabolism (R-HSA-8978868), mitochondrial fatty acid beta-oxidation (FAO) (R-HSA-77289), triglyceride metabolism (R-HSA-8979227), phospholipid metabolism (R-HSA-1483257), sphingolipid metabolism (R-HSA-428157), and aspartate and asparagine metabolism (R-HSA-74182).

Volcano Map

We selected all 47 genes in energy metabolism from the Pathcards web (https://pathCards.geencards.org) database and set up the threshold of the hub genes at a P value of less than 0.05, |logFC| of greater than 0.5 to screen for upregulated and downregulated genes of FECD. PPARα, encoding a translation factor, was identified.

Heatmap

We used the ComplexHeatmap package in R to analyze. A heatmap was drawn to visualize the correlation between genes involved in FAO and signaling by TGF-β family members (P < 0.05 were considered statistically significant).

Gene Set Variation Analysis (GSVA) and Correlation Analysis

GSVA is a powerful tool to detect subtle pathway activity changes over a sample population. GSVA analysis enhances the ability to detect subtle changes in pathway activity within the sample population compared with previous methods. The “GSVA” R package was used to calculate the scores of ferroptosis gene set and cuproptosis gene set. It was used to calculate the pathway score of each sample in GSE171830. The Pearson correlation analysis was used to analyze the correlation between FAO energy metabolism and signaling by TGFβ family members based on the FECD and control groups. We used the Corrplots package to visualize Pearson's correlation analysis result. The activation scores of the epithelial–mesenchymal transition pathway and FAO pathway were listed in Table 1.

Pathway Activation Scores in GSE171830

EMT, epithelial–mesenchymal transition.

Experimental Animals

C57BL/6 wild-type mice (specific pathogen-free animals, Beijing, China) and B6;129S4- PPARαtm1Gonz/J mice (The Jackson Laboratory, Farmington, CT, USA) were used for this study. The mice were housed in a climate-controlled environment and under a 12-hour light/dark cycle. Mice were anesthetized with 0.8% Pentobarbital sodium (40mg/kg) administered intraperitoneally. The age of PPARα−/− mice and wild-type mice ranged from 18 to 24 months. Female mice were used in the experiment. The 0.2% fenofibrate feed experiments and the intracameral injection of recombinant AAV-PPARα experiments were conducted on 2-month-old wild-type mice. All animal experiments were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the General Hospital of Chinese PLA and followed the ARVO Statement for the Use of Animals in Ophthalmic and Vision Research.

Identification of PPARαtm1Gonz/J Mouse Strain

We used the Mouse Tail Genotype Rapid Identification Kit (Beyotime, Shanghai, China) for mouse genotype identification. We cut the tip of the mouse tail of approximately 0.5 cm in length and placed it in the prepared digestive solution system and digest according to the procedure. After digestion was completed, we add 100 µL of Stop Solution to the sample, vortexed to mix and dilute it to 10 µM, and then added 1 µL to PCR reagents to start PCR reaction. We poured 0.5× TAE into the electrophoresis tank until the gel was covered, pulled out the comb, and then loaded the sample with 5 µL of marker and 10 µL of the sample into each well. The voltage is 170 V and run to the front end of the DNA to the 2/3 position. We took the gel block and developed it in the Bio-Rad imager with the program set to Nucleic Acid Gel.

Ultra-high-performance Liquid Chromatography Tandem Mass Spectrometry (UHPLC-MS/MS) Analysis

A single mouse cornea was counted as one sample. We collected a total of eight corneas of aged PPARα−/−mouse and eight corneas of aged wild-type mouse. Metabolomics profiling (Shanghai Bioprofile, Shanghai, China) was analyzed using a UPLC-ESI-Q-Orbitrap-MS system (UHPLC, Shimadzu Nexera X2 LC-30AD, Shimadzu, Japan) coupled with Q-Exactive Plus (Thermo Fisher Scientific, San Jose, CA, USA).

UV-A Irradiation of Mouse Cornea

The UV-A irradiation mouse model of late-onset FECD refer to the previous literature and were briefly described below. A UV-A LED light source (M365LP1; Thorlabs, Newton, NJ, USA) with an emission peak of 365 nm and full width at half maximum bandwidth of 9 nm was focused on the mouse's right cornea with a 4-mm-diameter spot after anaesthetizing. The time of UV-A exposure was adjusted to deliver the appropriate fluence (20 min 57 s for 500 J/cm2, 30 min 26 s for 750 J/cm2). The contralateral eye (left eye) served as an untreated control, which was protected by a sterile dressing.

Feeding With 0.2% Fenofibrate Feed

The 0.2% fenofibrate feed used in this experiment was ordered from Biopike Biotechnology (Beijing, China). Each kilogram of 0.2% fenofibrate feed contains 2 g of fenofibrate. Daily food intake of mice is 3 g, so the daily intake of fenofibrate is 6 mg, 0.2% fenofibrate feed dosage were determined in accordance with the literature. On the day of UV-A irradiation, mice were given feed containing 0.2% fenofibrate or ordinary feed. The cornea and corneal endothelium of the mice were observed 1 month after feeding.

Cell Culture and Cell Transfection

A normal human CEC line (B4G12 cells) was given by Shandong Eye Institute. Consistent with previous literature, cells were cultivated in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (Gibco, Carlsbad, CA, USA) containing 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (Gibco) and 50 U/mL penicillin and streptomycin antibiotics (Corning, Corning, NY, USA). All cells were cultured in a humidified atmosphere with a mixture of 1% O2, 5% CO2, and 94% N2 at 37°. Small interfering RNA against PPARα and nonspecific control small interfering RNA (GenePharma, Suzhou, China) were transfected into B4G12 cells using Lipofectamine RNAiMAX (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, USA) for 36 hours or 48 hours.

TGF-β Induces Damage to CECs (HCECs)

TGF-β (Proteintech, Rosemont, IL, USA) was dissolved in hydrochloric acid to prepare a solution of 100 µg/mL, and then Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium was added to prepare the mother solution of 10 µg/mL. When used, we added it to the complete medium at a ratio of 1/1000 to make the final concentration 10 ng/mL. When cells were passaged, the medium was replaced with the complete culture medium containing 10 ng/mL TGF-β, and the next experiment was performed after 24 hours of culture.

Slit-lamp Biomicroscope Examination

After anaesthetizing, the corneal opacities before UV-A and post-UV-A irradiation were measured, and the mice corneal photos were taken by a slit-lamp biomicroscope with a camera (Nikon D100, Tokyo, Japan). The corneal opacity scores ranged from 0 to 4. This scale was based on other research: 0, a normal cornea; 0.5, a slight haze visible only under a slit-lamp biomicroscope; 1, a mild haze; 2, a moderate haze with a visible iris; 3, a severe haze with an invisible iris; and 4, a severe haze with corneal ulceration.

Anterior Segment–Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) Examination

Anterior segment images, which show the corneal thickness, were taken by anterior segment–OCT (Optoprobe ISOCT, Wales, England) before UV-A irradiation and 1month after UV-A irradiation. Cross-sections were taken from the central cornea at 0° to 180°, 45° to 225°, 90° to 270°, and 135° to 315°, and the average central corneal thickness was calculated for each group (n = 6, after removing the maximum and minimum values).

Corneal In Vivo Confocal Microscopy

The mouse was anaesthetized and wrapped on a warm platform that holds the body securely. Corneal endothelium was observed by laser scanning IVCM using the Heidelberg Retina Tomograph III (HRT III) with Rostock Corneal Module (RCM) (Heidelberg Engineering, Heidelberg, Germany). before UV-A irradiation and after UV-A irradiation. Laser confocal microscopy acquires two-dimensional images that represent a coronal section of the cornea of 400 × 400 µm (160,000 µm2) at selectable corneal depths. Digital images were captured at a rate of 3 frames per second. The CE density of the Heidelberg Retina Tomograph (HRT) images was analyzed by a semiautomated cell counter in the software Heidelberg Eye Explorer, version 1.3.0 (Heidelberg Engineering GmbH) inbuilt in instrument.

Immunofluorescence Staining of Cornea Buttons

For cornea button staining, mouse corneal buttons were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min at room temperature. The cornea buttons were permeabilized and blocked in 10% Goat Serum (ZSGB-BIO, Beijing, China) with 0.1% Triton X-100 (Beyotime) for 2 hours. Then the cornea buttons were incubated with primary antibodies in 10% goat serum with 0.1% Triton X-100 overnight at 4°C, then incubated with goat anti-rabbit secondary antibodies (Invitrogen) for 2 hours. Antifade Mounting Medium with DAPI (ZSGB-BIO) was used, followed by covering a slide. The primary antibodies were used in Immunofluorescence are listed in Table 2.

Primary Antibodies Used in Immunofluorescence

Immunofluorescence Staining of Paraffin Section

The mouse was anesthetized with narcolan and then euthanized with cervical dislocation. Mouse eyeballs were removed and fixed in 10% formalin, then embedded in paraffin, sectioned to 2 µm, and stained with primary antibodies overnight at 4°C; then with goat anti-rabbit, mice or rat secondary antibodies (Invitrogen) for 2 hours. Antifade Mounting Medium with DAPI was used, followed by a slide. Digital images were obtained using a confocal microscope (FV3000-MCPSU, Olympus, Tokyo, Japan). The primary antibodies were used in Immunofluorescence are listed in Table 2. For CE density calculated from the images of three mice with ZO-1 immunostaining, cell count was performed manually by two blinded observers using ImageJ software.

Western Blot

B4G12 cells were collected by Cell scraper and lysed in RIPA buffer (Beyotime) with Protease and Phosphatase Inhibitors, Protein samples were separated by SDS-PAGE (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA) and transferred to PVDF-NC (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA). PVDF-NCs were blocked in 5% skimmed milk (Beyotime) and incubated in primary antibodies overnight at 4°C. β-Actin was used as a control. After incubation with horseradish peroxidase secondary anti-mouse or anti-rabbit antibodies (Beyotime); blot images were taken by enhanced chemiluminescence (Bio-Rad). The primary antibodies were used in Western blot are listed in Table 3.

Primary Antibody Used in Western Blot

Immunohistochemical Staining

Corneas were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, and the samples were then paraffin embedded and sectioned at a thickness of 2 µm for hematoxylin and eosin staining and Sirius red staining. Slides were viewed with an Olympus BX53 microscope equipped with a digital camera (Olympus).

Electron Microscope Section Preparation and Observation

Transmission Electron Microscope

Corneal specimens had been prefixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 1 h before trimmed into 1-mm strips and fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde overnight at 4°C, post-fixed with 1% osmium tetroxide, and then embed in Epon 812. After routine processing, the transmission electron microscope HITACHI HT7800 was used to observe corneal endothelium.

Scanning Electron Microscope

Corneal specimens had been prefixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 1 h before trimmed into 1-mm strips and fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde overnight at 4°C, post-fixed with 1% osmium tetroxide, and then embed in Epon 812. Semithin sections of 0.5- to 1.0-µm thickness were counterstained with toluidine blue/basic fuchsin, and examined under a light microscope. Ultrathin sections of 60 to 80 nm were collected on copper grids, doubled stained with uranyl acetate and lead citrate for 20 min each, then viewed and photographed with a transmission electron microscope. The thickness of the Descemet membrane was marked randomly by three vertical lines on each scanning electron microscope image.

Mitochondrial Membrane Potential Assay

The mitochondrial membrane potential was determined using the JC-1 Assay Kit (Beyotime, C2003S). JC-1 aggregates stay in mitochondria, which was shown as red. JC-1 monomer escapes into cytoplasm, which was shown as green, indicating the decreasing of mitochondrial membrane potential. Digital images were obtained using a confocal microscope (FV3000-MCPSU, Olympus).

Fatty Acid Oxidation Complete Assay

The extracellular oxygen consumption of mitochondrial FAO process was detected by Fatty Acid Oxidation Complete Assay Kit (Abcam, ab222944, Waltham, MA, USA). FCCP was added to induce the maximum oxygen consumption. Multifunctional microplate detector (Tecan, Spark, Morrisville, NC, USA) were used to detect the chemiluminescence.

Statistical Analysis

Data were shown as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance was estimated by the t test or F analysis using Prism. All the tests were two sided, and a P value of less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

Results

PPARα Is Identified as a Key Pathogenic Gene in FECD

Lipid metabolites were increased in the aqueous humor of patients with FECD, along with apolipoprotein E decreasing in corneal endothelium. To further investigate the major lipid metabolism changes occurring in FECD, GSEA was performed on the subsets of lipid metabolism (R-HSA-556833) of the FECD-related GSE171830 dataset. The analysis revealed significant downregulation of fatty acid metabolism (P = 0.0349) and mitochondrial FAO (P = 0.0029) pathways in the corneal endothelium of patients with FECD (Supplementary Fig. 1A). The mitochondrial FAO process is primarily associated with the TCA cycle and oxidative respiratory electron transport chain (OXPHOS) in mitochondria. Subsequently, GSEA analysis of the TCA cycle and OXPHOS were performed and this process showed significant inhibition in FECD (P = 0.0056) (Fig. 1A). To identify key genes, all genes involved in the FAO and TCA cycle pathways were extracted and DEGs were filtered based on a P value of less than 0.05 and a |logFC| of less than 0.5. PPARα, as one of the key downregulated energy metabolism genes in the FECD group, was identified (Fig. 1B). Other downregulated genes in FECD, including PPARGC1A, PPARGC1B, NCOA1, and ESRRA, are downstream genes regulated by PPARα (Fig. 1B). To further validate the impact of PPARα on corneal endothelium, we generated PPARα−/− mice and observed mild corneal opacity and endothelial damage in aged PPARα−/− mice (Fig. 1C).

PPARα is a key downregulated gene in the Pathogenesis of FECD. (A) In dataset GSE171830, GSEA reveals significant downregulation of mitochondrial FAO (P = 0.0029) and mitochondrial TCA cycle oxidative phosphorylation (P = 0.0056) pathways in FECD samples. (B) Volcano plot of all genes involved in mitochondrial FAO and mitochondrial TCA cycle oxidative phosphorylation processes, showing PPARα as a key downregulated gene. (C) Slit lamp photography, anterior segment OCT examination, confocal microscopy, and ZO-1 fluorescence staining of corneal endothelial morphology and density in aged PPARα−/− mice and wild-type mice exposed to 500 J/cm2 and 750 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation for 1 month. Scale bar, 20 µm. (D) Histogram of corneal opacity scores between different groups. (E) Histogram of corneal thickness between different groups. (F) Histogram of percent of Hexagonality endothelial cell between different groups. (G) Cell density between different groups. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001. (H) Immunofluorescence staining of PPARα in corneal, PPARα labeled with Alexa Fluor 488, cell nuclei labeled with DAPI. Scale bar, 20 µm.

Aged PPARα−/− Mice Exhibit Reduced Corneal Endothelial Function and Are More Susceptible to Corneal Endothelial Damage

The UV-A irradiation of mice cornea can simulate the pathophysiological changes of late-onset FECD clinically. We observed the phenotypes of aged PPARα−/− mice and wild-type mice 1 month after UV-A irradiation. Slit-lamp examination was used to assess the degree of corneal opacity in mice. The corneal opacity scores for each group of mice (n = 6, after removing the maximum and minimum values) were as follows: for wild-type mice, without UV-A irradiation, it was 0 ± 0; after 500 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 2.33 ± 0.47; after 750 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 2.8 ± 0.4; for PPARα−/− mice, without UV-A irradiation, it was 1.67 ± 0.47; after 500 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 3.17 ± 0.37; and after 750 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 3.8 ± 0.4. Under the same UV-A intensity, the corneal opacity in aged PPARα−/− mice was heavier than that in wild-type C57BL/6 mice (P < 0.05) (Fig. 1D). After 750 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, the iris and pupil of aged PPARα−/− mice were almost invisible in PPARα−/− mice (Fig. 1C). Anterior segment OCT was used to show the corneal thickness 1 month after UV-A irradiation. The statistical results of central corneal thickness for each group were as follows: for wild-type mice, without UV-A irradiation, it was 122.17 ± 4.49 µm; after 500 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 147.17 ± 12.47 µm; and after 750 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 201.60 ± 6.95 µm; for PPARα−/− mice, without UV-A irradiation, it was 181.17 ± 6.07 µm; after 500 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 207.83 ± 5.40 µm; and after 750 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 218.20 ± 5.84 µm (Fig. 1E). The corneal thickness was increased after irradiation (P < 0.05), At 500 J/cm2, the corneal thickness of PPARα−/− mice was thicker than that of wild-type mice, whereas at 750 J/cm2, there was no difference in cornea thickness between PPARα−/− mice and wild-type mice (P < 0.05). In vivo confocal microscopy observation and ZO-1 staining of mouse corneal buttons showed that UV-A could induce loss of regular hexagonal shape and an increase in cell size and shape variability (P < 0.05). For PPARα−/− mice, increases in the enlargement of cells and loss of regular hexagonal shape of the corneal endothelium were observed without UV-A irradiation (P < 0.05). After irradiation, the number of regular hexagonal CECs in PPARα−/− mice was significantly lower than that in wild-type mice (P < 0.05) (Fig. 1C). The number of regular hexagonal CECs was counted in five fields from the central cornea and the 12 o'clock, 3 o'clock, 6 o'clock, and 9 o'clock positions, respectively, for each group (n = 6, after removing the maximum and minimum values). For wild-type mice, without UV-A irradiation, it was 79.17 ± 4.95%; after 500 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 24.33 ± 3.82%; and after 750 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 19.0 ± 3.16%. For PPARα−/− mice without UV-A irradiation, it was 15.5 ± 3.5%; after 500 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 5.17 ± 3.08%; after 750 J/cm2 UV-A irradiation, it was 1.08 ± 0.52% (Figs. 1F, 1G). Immunofluorescence staining of cornea showed that after irradiation at 750 J/cm2, PPARα expression was weaker in wild-type mice (Fig. 1H).

The Corneal Metabolites Changes of Aged PPARα−/− Mice Collaborating With Enzymes Alteration in FECD Cases of GSE171830

To further explore the relationship between corneal PPARα alterations and corneal metabolism, we detected metabolites in the corneas of aged PPARα−/− mice and wild-type mice (n = 8) by using UHPLC-MS/MS. Principal component analysis (OPLS-DA analysis) showed that the metabolites in the corneas of PPARα−/− mice belonged to the different group from those of wild-type mice, which exhibited a unique metabolic profile (Supplementary Fig. 2A). The top three differential metabolites between PPARα−/− mice and wild-type mice were fatty acyls (17.39%), carbohydrates (13.04%), and polyketides (13.04%) (Supplementary Fig. 2B). To further investigate changes in metabolites between PPARα−/− mice and wild-type mice, a volcano plot was shown, with differential metabolites marking (|log2 FC| > 1; P < 0.05) (Supplementary Fig. 2C). Downregulated lipid metabolites in PPARα−/− mice included acylcarnitines (including acetylcarnitine, butyrylcarnitine, and propionylcarnitine) and long-chain fatty acids (including LPE18:2, LPE18:1, peanut oil acid, docosatetraenoic acid, and deoxycholic acid). Long-chain fatty acids can provide materials for FAO. Acetylcarnitine, propionylcarnitine, butyrylcarnitine, and acylcarnitines are metabolites during the process of mitochondrial FAO (Fig. 2). Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes analysis of differential metabolites revealed significant decreases in fatty acid metabolism and fatty acid degradation in PPARα−/− mice (Supplementary Fig. 2D), consistent with decreasing FAO-related metabolic enzymes in the FECD samples of GSE171830 dataset (Fig. 2). The FAO process involves the entry of fatty acids into mitochondria to generate acetyl CoA, which then enters the TCA cycle. In the GSE171830 dataset, the corneal endothelium of patients with FECD showed a decrease in enzymes involved in the FAO process, except for CPT2 and SLC25A20, which allow long-chain fatty acids to enter the mitochondrial matrix from the mitochondrial interstitium. The corneal endothelium of aged PPARα−/− mice also presented inhibition of FAO metabolic processes, manifested as reduced uptake of long-chain fatty acids and ultimately reduced production of acetylcarnitine.

Integrated analysis of metabolomics in corneas of PPARα−/− mice and enzymes changes in GSE171830. The color scale represents log fold change (logFC), with blue indicating downregulation, red indicating upregulation, and darker colors indicating greater |logFC| and more significant differences. The size of the circles represents the P value significance level. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001. The metabolites are represented using box diagram, and the metabolic enzymes are represented using scatter plots. Acyl-CoA, acetyl coenzyme A; FFA, free fatty acid; WT, wild type.

PPARα Knockout Activated Epithelial–Mesenchymal Transition in Mouse CECs and Human CEC Lines

One of the primary pathological changes in FECD is thickening of the Descemet membrane and the formation of guttae, which subsequently leads to damage and apoptosis of CECs. We further investigated whether the impairment of FAO process is related to the production of abnormal ECM in corneal endothelium. We used GSVA analysis to calculate the activation scores of FAO and ECM remodeling pathways for each sample in the GSE171830 dataset (Table 1). The negative correlation between FAO energy supply and ECM remodeling was indicated in the GSE171830 dataset by Pearson correlation analysis on GSVA scores (R = −0.6452; P = 0.0235) (Fig. 3A). The correlation heatmap also showed negative correlations between genes involved in FAO energy supply and genes related to ECM remodeling (Fig. 3B). Additionally, alpha smooth muscle actin (α-SMA) expression was observed in CECs and stromal cells of postirradiation mice and PPARα−/− mice (Figs. 3C, 3D) (P < 0.05). In patients with FECD, Col1a1 is the main collagen in the Descemet membrane and stroma. Col1a1 is rarely expressed in normal cornea, but is elevated in the corneas of postirradiation mice and PPARα−/− mice (Figs.  3C, 3E) (P < 0.05). After transfection of B4G12 with small interfering PPARα, Western blotting was used to detect the expression of PPARα, α-SMA, Col1a, Na+-K+-ATPase, and ZO-1 in B4G12 cells. The results showed that knockdown of PPARα increased the expression of α-SMA and Col1a, while decreasing the expression of Na+-K+-ATPase and ZO-1 (Figs. 3F, 3G). These results indicated that the downregulation of PPARα leads to fibrotic changes in corneal endothelium.

PPARα affects corneal endothelial fibrosis through regulating corneal endothelial FAO process. (A) Correlation analysis of GSVA activation scores for FAO and epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT) pathways in GSE171830 dataset (R = −0.6452; P = 0.0235). (B) Correlation analysis of key genes involved in FAO and EMT; blue indicates a negative correlation and red indicates a positive correlation. (C) Immunofluorescence staining of aged PPARα−/− mice and wild-type mice before and after exposing to 500 J/cm2 and 750 J/cm2 irradiation for 1 month, with α-SMA and COL1a labeled with Alexa Fluor 488, and cell nuclei stained with DAPI, Scale bar, 20 µm. (D) Histogram of α-SMA–positive cells (%). (E) The histogram of COL1a1 immunofluorescence intensity. n = 6,*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001. (F) Western blot detection of α-SMA, Col1a1, Na+-K+-ATPase, and ZO-1 in PPARα-knockdown B4G12 cell line. (G) The histogram of grayscale values obtained from western blot, n = 3, *P < 0.05.

Overexpression or Activation of PPARα in CECs Alleviated Corneal Endothelial Damage

To further validate whether PPARα has a protective effect on CECs, we conducted the following experiments: mice were fed with regular feed or feed containing 0.2% fenofibrate (a PPARα agonist). The right eye of the mice was irradiated with 750 J/cm2 UV-A, and the left eye was covered as a control. One month after UV-A irradiation, we found that the group fed with 0.2% fenofibrate feed showed decreased corneal edema and opacity compared with the group fed with regular feed. After irradiation, CECs exhibited enlargement, irregular cell morphology, and decreased cell density in regular feed group. However, in the group fed with 0.2% fenofibrate feed, there was no significant decrease in cell density and the cell size was uniform, but there was an increase in intercellular spaces. This finding indicates that oral administration of 0.2% fenofibrate has a protective and therapeutic effect on CECs (Figs. 4A–C). Hematoxylin and eosin staining and picrosirius red staining were used to observe cell damage and collagen changes in the cornea. After irradiation, on hematoxylin and eosin examination of regular feed group, the CEC thickness decreased, cell density decreased with deepened nuclear staining, and the corneal epithelial layer became thinner (P < 0.001), while the stromal layer significantly thickened (approximately 90 µm; P < 0.001). However, in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group, CEC nuclei were not condensed significantly, and the endothelial cell layer thickness was maintained, as well as epithelium thickness (P < 0.001), the stromal thickness was reduced compared with the group with UV-A irradiation alone (P < 0.001) (Figs. 5A–C). In the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group, the corneal epithelial cells were thinner than that in the normal group, although there were no differences before and after UV-A irradiation, which may be related to factors such as reduced body weight in the fenofibrate-fed group. On picrosirius red staining, we only observed a distinct Descemet membrane in the group with UV-A irradiation alone, possibly related to the thickening of the Descemet membrane.

To further validate the protective effect of PPARα on the corneal endothelium. We used AAV9-shPPARα to overexpress PPARα in CECs by intracameral injection. After 1 month of injection, the fluorescence intensity of PPARα protein in shPPARα group was higher than that in shNC group (Supplementary Figs. 3A, 3D). To further confirm the overexpression of PPARα, corneal endothelium tissues were collected (10 per group) for Western blot. The results showed that intracameral injection of AAV9-shPPARα increased the expression of PPARα in CECs (Supplementary Figs. 3B, 3C). Subsequently, we conducted the following experiments to investigate the effect of PPARα on corneal endothelium: the groups were divided into control, UV-A irradiation, UV-A + shNC, and UV-A + shPPARα groups. UV-A irradiation was performed with an energy of 750 J/cm2. One month after irradiation, we found that corneal opacity and edema were reduced after overexpression of PPARα (Figs. 4D, 4F).

Overexpression or Activation of PPARα in CECs Inhibits Abnormal ECM Accumulation

Transmission electron microscopy was used to observe the effect of PPARα overexpression on the thickness of Descemet membrane. Scanning electron microscopy was used to observe changes in CEC structure and intercellular connections. we conducted the following experiments to investigate the effect of PPARα on corneal endothelium: the groups were divided into control, UV-A irradiation, UV-A + shNC, and UV-A + shPPARα groups. UV-A irradiation was performed with an energy of 750 J/cm2. One month after irradiation, the thickness of the Descemet membrane in the UV-A irradiation group and the UV-A + shNC group was significantly greater compared with the control group, also with abnormal ECM accumulation presenting two layers. The Descemet membrane in the UV-A + shPPARα group showed no significant thickening compared with the control group (P < 0.001) (Figs. 67A, 6C). We also performed transmission electron microscopy on the Descemet membrane of mice fed with 0.2% fenofibrate feed or regular feed. One month after irradiation, the thickness of the Descemet membrane increased in mice fed by the regular feed, whereas it decreased to normal Descemet membrane thickness in 0.2% fenofibrate feed group (P < 0.001) (Supplementary Fig. 4A).

(B) Scanning electron microscopy was performed to observe the structure of intercellular junctions and microvilli (n = 6). Scare bar, 10 µm. (C) Histogram of the thickness of the Descemet membrane. Guttae typically occur at the junctions of CECs, disrupting intercellular connections and subsequently leading to corneal endothelial apoptosis. The microvilli structure of CECs is an important structure for absorbing and transporting nutrients. As shown in Figure 6B, after irradiation, some intercellular connections between the corneal endothelium were disrupted, leading to widened intercellular gaps, and the microvillus on the cell surface were reduced in both UV-A irradiation and UV-A + shNC groups (Fig. 6D). However, the UV-A + shPPARα group showed intact intercellular connections. The density of microvilli was slightly reduced compared with the control group (P < 0.001), but the density and morphology of microvilli were better preserved compared with the UV-A irradiation and UV-A + shNC groups. This finding indicates that the overexpression or activation of PPARα in CECs can inhibit corneal endothelial damage significantly, maintain cell function, and suppress thickening of Descemet membrane.

Activation of PPARα Significantly Inhibits Corneal Endothelial Fibrosis

Because the Descemet membrane is secreted by CECs, we examined the fibrosis of CECs. To further explore the mechanism by which PPARα inhibits abnormal ECM secretion, we conducted immunofluorescence staining and Western blot of whole corneal button. S100A4 is associated with various cellular activities and is also considered a marker of cellular endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition. UV-A irradiation was performed with an energy of 750 J/cm2. One month after irradiation, PPARα was decreased in corneal endothelium, whereas S100A4 expression was increased in the corneal endothelium. However, PPARα expression was increased significantly in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group and S100A4 expression had no change compared with regular feed group (Figs. 6A–C). This finding indicates that the activation of PPARα does not affect S100A4 expression in CECs. Through ZO-1 staining, we found that, in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group, after irradiation, consistent with elevated PPARα, the enlargement of endothelial cells was alleviated, the cell junction remained relatively intact (Fig. 7A). Subsequently, we detected fibrosis-related proteins and FAO-related enzymes in the corneal endothelium by using Western blot. One month after irradiation, FAO-related enzymes, PPARα, Cpt1a, and ACADM were decreased in regular feed group, whereas they were increased in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group compared with the control group. The mitochondrial biosynthesis-related gene, Tfam, was decreased after irradiation; compared with the regular feed group, Tfam was increased in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group before and after irradiation. After irradiation, fibrosis factors, Col1a1 and α-SMA, were both increased in the regular feed group, whereas there was no change in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group (Figs. 7D, 7E). These results indicated that fenofibrate treatment significantly enhanced the FAO process, maintained mitochondrial homeostasis, and inhibited the fibrosis process of CECs.

Fenofibrate Can Inhibit Corneal Endothelial Fibrosis and Protect Mitochondria Oxidative Respiration In Vitro

We treated B4G12 cells with 10 ng/mL of TGF-β for 24 hours to induce fibrosis, and the TGF-β + Feno group also received 30 µM fenofibrate simultaneous. The groups were divided into control, Feno, TGF-β, and TGF-β + Feno groups. Under light microscopy, B4G12 cells in the TGF-β group seemed to be elongated with disrupted cell–cell connections, presenting a fibroblast morphology. In contrast, cells in the TGF-β + Feno group exhibited uniform and regular polygonal morphology with tight cell–cell connections. The Feno group showed cell morphology closer to pentagonal or hexagonal shapes compared with the control group (Fig. 8A). Confocal microscopy analysis of the fibrosis marker FN revealed that the fluorescence intensity in the TGF-β group was increased significantly compared with the control and Feno groups. However, the fluorescence intensity in the TGF-β + Feno group was significantly weaker compared with the TGF-β group, and the Feno group showed weaker fibronectin fluorescence compared with the control group (Figs. 8B, 8E). E-cadherin (E-cad) and N-cadherin (N-cad) are involved in maintaining cell–cell adhesion and the polygonal morphology of B4G12 cells. During fibrosis, the polygonal morphology of cell is lost, and E-cad and N-cad translocate from the cell membrane to the cytoplasm. Confocal microscopy analysis of E-cad and α-SMA staining in B4G12 cells showed that, in the TGF-β group, E-cad was translocated in the cytoplasm, whereas in the TGF-β + Feno group, E-cad was distributed on the cell membrane. Additionally, α-SMA in the TGF-β group was increased in the cell cytoskeleton, whereas in the TGF-β + Feno group, α-SMA was distributed mainly in cell gap (Fig. 8C). Confocal microscopy analysis of N-cad and S100A4 staining in B4G12 cells showed that N-cad in the TGF-β group was distributed in the cytoplasm, whereas in the TGF-β + Feno group, N-cad was distributed mainly on the cell membrane. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of S100A4 in the TGF-β group was stronger than that in the TGF-β + Feno group (Figs. 8D, 8F). These results indicate that fenofibrate can inhibit TGF-β–induced fibrosis. Next, we investigated whether fenofibrate affects mitochondrial function and consequently impacts fibrosis in B4G12 cells. JC1 staining of B4G12 cells revealed that TGF-β caused damage to the mitochondrial membrane potential, leading to the presence of JC1 monomers in the cytoplasm, showing green fluorescence. However, fenofibrate treatment reduced the damage to the mitochondrial membrane potential induced by TGF-β, resulting in a reduction in JC1 monomers. Subsequently, we measured mitochondrial oxygen consumption and found that treatment with fenofibrate significantly increased the maximal oxygen consumption induced by FCCP with or without TGF-β (Figs. 9A–C).

Fenofibrate suppresses TGF-β–induced fibrosis in B4G12 cells. (A) We treated B4G12 cells with 10 ng/mL of TGF-β for 24 h to induce fibrosis, and the TGF-β + Feno group received 30 µM fenofibrate simultaneous. The groups were divided into control, Feno, TGF-β, and TGF-β + Feno groups. B4G12 cells were photographed under white light. Arrowheads indicate cell morphology. (B) Fibronectin (FN) in B4G12 was stained by Alexa Fluor 568. FN is shown in red, and DAPI staining for nuclei is shown in blue. Scale bar, 50 µm. (C) α-SMA in B4G12 was stained by Alexa Fluor 568. E-cad in B4G12 was stained by Alexa Fluor 488. Scale bar, 50 µm. (D) N-cad in B4G12 was stained by Alexa Fluor 568. S100A4 in B4G12 was stained by Alexa Fluor 488. Scale bar, 50 µm. (E) Quantification of FN fluorescence intensity in B4G12. (F) Quantification of S100a4 fluorescence intensity in B4G12. n = 3. *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001.

Discussion

The corneal endothelium is the key tissue responsible for maintaining corneal transparency, with its primary function being the transportation of nutrients from the aqueous humor to the cornea and the regulation of fluid from the stroma to the anterior chamber. CECs have a high demand for ATP; thus, the density of mitochondria in the corneal endothelium ranks second only to that of photoreceptor cells. This study for the first time proposes that the mitochondrial FAO–TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation are significantly impaired in FECD through GSEA analysis of GSE171830. Furthermore, the key downregulated gene of energy metabolism in the FECD dataset, PPARα, primarily regulates the FAO process. Through in vivo experiments involving PPARα−/− mice, PPARα overexpression, and activation of PPARα by fenofibrate, it was demonstrated that PPARα exerts a protective effect on the corneal endothelium and can inhibit abnormal thickening of the Descemet membrane.

A meta-analysis of RNA sequencing datasets of FECD corneal endothelium demonstrated that both mitochondrial functions and energy-related processes were decreased in FECD cases with or without the CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion. Except the OXPHOS decreasing in FECD cases, our study also marks the first observation in FECD cases of decreased mitochondrial FAO process, which conducted the acetyl-coenzyme A carboxylase for TCA cycle and OXPHOS. Previous studies related to mitochondrial function and energy production in corneal endothelium mostly focus on the function of SLC4A11. Hamuro et al. reported that MiR-34a induced the polarization of mitochondrial respiration to OXPHOS, after cell-state transition. In our study, an analysis of all genes involved in energy metabolism revealed that PPARα is the most significantly downregulated gene in FECD. We generated PPARα−/− mice and found that there were mild corneal opacity and endothelial damage in aged PPARα−/− mice. Under the same intensity of UV-A irradiation, the corneal opacity in aged PPARα−/− mice was heavier and corneas were thicker than that in wild-type C57BL/6 mice. For ZO-1 staining and in vivo confocal microscopy, the number of regular hexagonal CECs in PPARα−/− mice was significantly lower than that in wild-type mice. In addition, the corneal metabolites changes of PPARα−/− mice collaborating with enzymes alteration in FECD cases of GSE171830. Overexpression by intracameral injection AAV-shPPARα or activation of PPARα by feeding 0.2% fenofibrate feed alleviated corneal endothelial damage after irradiation, shown as maintaining cell density and cell morphology, reducing corneal edema and opacity. However, PPARα overexpression or activation had a limited effect on the loss of microvillus and the enlargement of intercellular spaces. Thus, we identified that PPARα was a protective factor for corneal endothelium in FECD.

The fibrotic process associated with guttae formation is the key factor leading to the decrease in CEC density in FECD. Currently, there is no method to suppress guttae generation. In our study, we observed that PPARα can inhibit the thickening of Descemet membrane in a UV-A–irradiated mice late-onset FECD model. Using GSVA, we found a negative correlation between corneal endothelial fibrosis and the FAO process. Furthermore, we observed that fenofibrate or overexpression of PPARα significantly inhibited the thickening of the Descemet membrane and the accumulation of abnormal fibers in Descemet membrane induced by UV-A irradiation. After irradiation, the expression of expression of α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA) and Col1a1 in 0.2% fenofibrate fed mice was decreased, thereby inhibiting the process of corneal fibrosis. In addition, PPARα can protect the mitochondria from injury, increase mitochondria production and active the FAO process. Fenofibrate treatment in B4G12 reduced the destruction of the mitochondrial membrane potential induced by TGF-β. Mitochondrial biosynthesis related gene, Tfam, was decreased after irradiation; compared with the regular feed group, Tfam was increased in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group before or after irradiation. After irradiation, the FAO-related enzymes, Cpt1a and ACADM, were decreased in the regular feed group, whereas they were increased in the 0.2% fenofibrate feed group compared with control group. Fenofibrate can also increase the maximal oxygen consumption induced by FCCP with or without TGF-β treatment. Previous studies have reported that regulating key enzymes in the FAO process can inhibit TGF-β-induced fibrosis. Chung et al. reported that the exaggerated lipid accumulated in kidneys of aged PPARα−/− mice with decreased activity of the FAO pathway and a severe fibrosis. Gallage et al. reported that the combined knockdown of PPARα and Pck1 in vivo abolished the beneficial outcomes of fasting against inflammation and fibrosis via activation of a PI3K/AKT/PDGF axis. Moreover, overexpression of Pck1 alone or together with PPARα in vivo lowered hepatic triglycerides and steatosis. Both of these experiments were consistent with the phenomenon we have observed in corneal endothelium. In our study, we sought to explain how PPARα inhibits the fibrosis process. We tested the expression of S100A4 in our study, a damage-associated molecular pattern protein involved in fibrotic responses. After irradiation, the expression of S100A4 was increased with or without fenofibrate treatment. This finding indicates that S100A4 is not the target of PPARα in inhibiting the fibrosis of endothelium.

The cellular cytoskeleton is a crucial structure responsible for sensing and regulating the ECM. It serves as a mediator for the interaction between cells and the ECM, facilitating the deposition, rearrangement, and degradation of the ECM by transmitting signals into the cell. The dynamic structure of the cytoskeleton is affected by changes in energy supply, and the physiological function of cells depends on the balance between the assembly and disassembly of the cytoskeleton with other types of proteins. An insufficient energy supply disrupts the balance of cytoskeletal signaling, leading to the deposition and structural disorganization of the ECM. In our study, after irradiation, compared with the AAV-shNC group, overexpression of corneal endothelial PPARα resulted in a more regular arrangement of cell–cell connections as observed by scanning electron microscopy, with no apparent disruption of cell connections. Additionally, in vitro experiments demonstrated that administration of fenofibrate significantly inhibited the occurrence of fibrosis induced by TGF-β. E-cad and N-cad are important factors in maintaining the pentagonal-hexagonal morphology of CECs. Previous studies have reported that, during CEC fibrosis, E-cad and N-cad redistribute from the cell membrane to the cytoplasm. In vitro experiments showed that the administration of fenofibrate inhibited the redistribution of E-cad and N-cad to the cytoplasm, which was induced by TGF-β.

Although PPARα activation partially mitigates mitochondrial dysfunction and fibrosis, there still were some limitations of PPARα activation as a therapeutic strategy. First, PPARα activation did not preserve the microvilli structure and cell–cell junctions fully. In future treatments, it may be possible to combine this agent with the other drugs, which could save microvilli loss and increase intercellular space. In addition, the development of topical ocular administration of fenofibrate will avoid the liver and kidney toxicity of oral administration.

In conclusion, our study demonstrates that the decline of PPARα is an important reason for the occurrence of FECD. Perturbation of PPARα signaling may disturb mitochondria fatty acid oxidation (FAO) process, then cause corneal endothelium fibrosis and producing abnormal ECM in Descemet membrane (Fig. 9D). Overexpression or activation of PPARα can inhibit the thickening of Descemet membrane, which is the primary pathology in FECD.

Fenofibrate protected mitochondrial integrity and mitochondrial FAO in B4G12 with TGF-β treatment.
